narrative:
Sen. Mike Rounds brings to the U.S. Senate the same managerial finesse he demonstrated as Governor of South Dakota, where he oversaw the EB-5 visa scandal — a program that was supposed to trade green cards for economic investment but instead resulted in a bankrupt beef packing plant, vanished foreign investor funds, and a state official's suspicious death. Rounds submitted incorrect information to investigators, his EB-5 czar lied to federal officials, and the feds eventually killed South Dakota's program entirely. Naturally, voters rewarded this with a Senate seat in 2014. The American Dream lives.

Now comfortably ensconced on the Senate Armed Services Committee — where he chairs the Cybersecurity Subcommittee and was ranked the 6th most effective senator on defense issues — Rounds has become a reliable advocate for the Pentagon's interests. He has received over $200,000 from oil, gas, and coal interests since 2012, and his defense-heavy committee portfolio ensures the military-industrial complex has a dependable friend in Pierre's favorite son. An audit of the Pentagon? That would be like auditing his own fan mail.

On Social Security, Rounds offered a "stark warning" in 2023 that Congress must act to "protect" the program — Washington code for raising the retirement age or means-testing benefits rather than, say, lifting the payroll tax cap on the wealthy. He has shown zero interest in the 80% Bill's proposal to fund Social Security by taxing ultra-wealthy Americans. When it comes to Medicare drug negotiation, banning corporate PACs, dark money disclosure, or paid family leave, Rounds has been conspicuously absent from the fight. He even proposed abolishing the Department of Education in November 2024, because apparently what South Dakota's rural schools need is less federal support.

Rounds occupies five Senate committees — Appropriations, Armed Services, Banking, Indian Affairs, and Intelligence — giving him an extraordinary perch from which to do extraordinarily little for the populist policies 80% of Americans support. He's the senator who warns you the house is on fire while pocketing checks from the arsonists. Trump endorsed his reelection in July 2025, which tells you everything about whose interests Rounds actually serves. South Dakotans deserve a senator who fights for them, not one who treats the EB-5 playbook as a career template.

Jamie Raskin is that rare creature in Congress: a constitutional law professor who actually appears to have read the Constitution. Representing Maryland's 8th District since 2017, Raskin arrived on Capitol Hill with three decades of legal scholarship and a reputation for treating the Bill of Rights as something more than decorative parchment [1]. He promptly put that background to work, becoming one of the most vocal proponents of government transparency and anti-corruption legislation in the House. His support for banning congressional stock trading, eliminating dark money in elections, and requiring the DISCLOSE Act suggests he believes public servants should serve the public — a position that remains, somehow, controversial [2].

Raskin's legislative record on the 80% Bill's priorities reads like a checklist of popular policies that Washington inexplicably struggles to pass. He has championed Medicare drug price negotiation, paid family leave, and closing tax loopholes that allow billionaires to pay lower effective rates than their secretaries [3]. He co-sponsored the Pentagon audit push, apparently operating under the radical assumption that an institution spending $800 billion annually should be able to account for where the money goes [4]. His backing of fair elections reform and the ban on corporate PACs aligns with his longstanding argument that democracy works better when voters, rather than corporate treasuries, pick the winners.

On issues of accountability, Raskin has been characteristically blunt. He supported the release of the Epstein files, police body camera mandates, and lobbying bans for former members of Congress — positions that earned him few friends in the Capitol Hill cocktail circuit but considerable credibility with constituents [5]. His push for term limits and junk fee bans reflects a broader philosophy that institutions, whether governmental or corporate, tend to calcify when left unchecked. He has argued that the revolving door between Congress and K Street is less a door than a conveyor belt, and his voting record backs up the rhetoric [6].

Perhaps most notably, Raskin became a household name as the lead impeachment manager in Donald Trump's second impeachment trial in 2021, delivering arguments just weeks after the death of his son Tommy — a performance that even his political opponents acknowledged as extraordinary [7]. That experience hardened his commitment to democratic norms and government reform. His alignment with the 80% Bill's twenty planks is not an accident of political convenience but a reflection of a career spent arguing that popular, common-sense policies should not require an act of political courage to support [8]. In a Congress where the median member's boldest stance is often choosing which lobbyist's fundraiser to attend, Raskin remains stubbornly committed to the idea that representatives should actually represent.

Jacky Rosen is the Senate's answer to the question: "What if a computer programmer optimized her career for the exact center of the Democratic Party?" A former synagogue president and software developer, Rosen won her Senate seat in 2018 and coasted to re-election in 2024, positioning herself as a pragmatic moderate who can work across the aisle [1]. Her bipartisan brand is real -- she co-introduced prescription drug pricing bills with Republicans John Cornyn and John Curtis [2] -- but so is the money behind it.

AIPAC is Rosen's single largest donor, contributing over $1 million to her campaigns [3]. Securities and investment firms led the industry contributions to her Victory Fund at $635,570 in 2024, with hedge funds also ranking among her top industry backers [4]. The Meruelo Group, a Las Vegas casino and media conglomerate, topped her Victory Fund donors at $59,700 [5]. None of this is illegal. All of it is instructive.

On the 80% Bill's platform, Rosen's record is genuinely strong on prescription drugs. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which enabled Medicare to negotiate drug prices for the first time [6], and she's championed the $2,000 out-of-pocket cap for seniors [7]. She co-sponsored the repeal of the 2002 Iraq AUMF, aligning with the bill's 'End Forever Wars' article [8]. But her record gets thinner on the structural reform issues that would actually change how Washington works.

On the DISCLOSE Act -- which would require immediate disclosure of big donors and trace money through shell companies -- Rosen has not been a cosponsor, an odd gap for someone whose own donor list reads like a hedge fund conference attendee roster [9]. She has not championed congressional term limits or a stock-trading ban. She earned a 90% score on a 2024 retiree voting record [10], which sounds impressive until you realize that protecting Social Security is about the safest position a Nevada Democrat can take given the state's large retiree population.

Rosen is not a villain. She's something more common and, in some ways, more frustrating: a senator who will reliably vote for popular things that already have momentum while carefully avoiding the fights that might upset the donor class. The 80% Bill asks whether "bipartisan" means working for the people or just finding the safest spot in the room.

John Rutherford is a Republican representing Florida's 5th Congressional District, which covers southeast Jacksonville and St. Johns County. Before arriving in Congress in 2017, Rutherford spent 41 years at the Jacksonville Sheriff's Office, eventually serving three terms as Duval County Sheriff from 2003 to 2015 [1]. He now sits on the powerful House Appropriations Committee, including the Homeland Security and Military Construction subcommittees [2] — a perch that gives him direct influence over where billions in federal dollars flow, and, as it turns out, a front-row seat for some remarkably well-timed personal investing.

On the twenty planks of the 80% Bill, Rutherford's record is best described as strategically silent. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, end dark money, require Pentagon audits, impose a lifetime lobbying ban, or pass the DISCLOSE Act. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the Medicare drug price negotiation provisions supported by roughly 80% of Americans [3]. He has taken no public action on paid family leave, banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, closing the carried interest loophole, banning junk fees, or enacting right-to-repair protections. On ending forever wars through AUMF repeal, Rutherford — a law-and-order Republican who never met a defense budget he didn't want to increase — has shown no interest in reclaiming congressional war-making authority.

Where Rutherford's record turns from unremarkable to instructive is in the stock trading department. On February 24, 2022 — the day Russia invaded Ukraine — Rutherford purchased shares in Raytheon, the defense contractor that manufactures the Javelin and Stinger missiles Western allies were rushing to Kyiv [4]. He then voted for a $40 billion supplemental military aid package for Ukraine, from which Raytheon stood to profit handsomely [5]. His office explained the trades were made by a third-party manager of his individual retirement account [4]. In 2023, his stock portfolio returned 69.1%, placing him among the top ten best-performing traders in Congress and dramatically outperforming the broader market [6]. For a man who has not cosponsored a single stock-trading ban, his portfolio speaks volumes about why such a ban might be necessary.

Follow the money further and the picture sharpens. Rutherford's campaign donors include defense contractors Boeing, Lockheed Martin, and General Dynamics, along with the National Rifle Association and the National Apartment Association [7]. These are the very industries that benefit from his Appropriations Committee votes and his refusal to support reforms like banning corporate PACs, auditing the Pentagon, or restricting corporate homebuying. His donor base reads less like a list of supporters and more like a client roster.

John Rutherford spent four decades in law enforcement telling people to follow the rules. In Congress, he has discovered that the rules are considerably more optional when you are the one writing them — and trading on them. His silence on nearly every plank of the 80% Bill is not an oversight; it is a business model.

Guy Reschenthaler is the kind of congressman who signs pledges the way most people sign up for free trials — with every intention of canceling before the commitment kicks in. The Republican Chief Deputy Whip from Pennsylvania's 14th district has spent four terms in Congress building a reputation as a reliable party soldier who will say whatever sounds tough on camera and vote however leadership tells him to behind closed doors.

Reschenthaler's most illuminating moment came courtesy of a billboard. In September 2025, U.S. Term Limits erected a sign in his district calling him out for breaking his pledge to support a constitutional amendment limiting House members to three two-year terms. He had cosponsored the term limits bill in each of his first three terms, then quietly stopped in his fourth — the term that would have been his last under the very amendment he championed. Nearly 100 House members, including five of his fellow Pennsylvania Republicans, continued to cosponsor. Reschenthaler apparently decided that term limits are a wonderful idea for everyone except Guy Reschenthaler.

On January 6th, Reschenthaler voted against certifying the electoral votes from both Arizona and Pennsylvania, joining 147 Republicans in rejecting the election results. He later called his vote "symbolic," explaining that "everybody knew that vote was not going to overturn the results of the election." A symbolic vote to undermine democratic certification is still a vote to undermine democratic certification — the adjective does not improve the noun.

As Chief Deputy Whip, Reschenthaler's job is essentially to count votes and enforce party discipline, which makes him one of the most connected fundraisers in the House Republican conference. His campaign brought in $596,700 in a recent fundraising disclosure, and he cruised to reelection in 2024 with 66.6% of the vote in his safely gerrymandered district. When your seat is that secure, the fundraising is not for survival — it is for influence.

Reschenthaler served as a Navy JAG officer in Iraq, where he prosecuted terrorists in Baghdad and helped defend Navy SEALs in a high-profile case. It is a genuinely impressive service record that he leverages to sit on the Judiciary and Foreign Affairs committees. But military service and government accountability are apparently two different things in his mind — he has shown no meaningful interest in auditing the Pentagon, reforming defense spending, or supporting the DISCLOSE Act.

On stock trading, financial disclosure trackers show Reschenthaler has made trades as recently as December 2024, though the volume appears modest. On Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, lobbying bans, dark money reform, and corporate PAC restrictions, Reschenthaler's record is defined by what is missing: any indication he has ever considered crossing his party leadership on a single structural reform that 80% of Americans support.

The whip counts the votes. He does not rock the boat.

John Rose is a $48-million man sitting on the House Financial Services and Agriculture Committees — the two committees with the most direct power over industries that made him rich. Before Congress, Rose co-founded Transcender Corp., an IT certification company sold for $60 million in 2000, and he still runs Boson Software. He also operates his family's 230-year-old farm in DeKalb County. It is a remarkable coincidence that a man whose fortune spans agriculture and finance landed on the exact two committees that regulate agriculture and finance.

Rose's voting record is a study in performative fiscal conservatism deployed at strategically useless moments. In May 2019, he single-handedly blocked a $19.1 billion disaster relief bill during a pro forma session — aid for Americans hit by hurricanes, floods, and wildfires — citing concerns about the national deficit. The same deficit concern evaporated when it came time to support tax cuts that benefit the wealthy. He later voted against the Fiscal Responsibility Act of 2023, joining 70 other Republicans who thought the bipartisan deal to avoid a catastrophic debt default was insufficiently pure.

On January 6th-adjacent matters, Rose's record is particularly revealing. He voted to overturn the 2020 election results in Arizona and Pennsylvania. He joined the Supreme Court lawsuit seeking to discard millions of votes in four swing states. And when Congress moved to honor the Capitol Police officers who defended the building during the insurrection, Rose was one of just 21 Republicans to vote against giving them the Congressional Gold Medal — saying it was "too soon." Too soon to thank the people who took beatings so Congress could certify an election Rose was trying to overturn.

On the 80% Bill's planks, Rose offers nothing. No support for banning congressional stock trading (his net worth makes that personal), no interest in dark money disclosure, no movement on corporate PAC bans, term limits, or Pentagon audits. He sits on Financial Services but has championed no consumer protection measures — no junk fee bans, no right to repair, no corporate homebuying restrictions.

Now Rose is running for governor of Tennessee in 2026, with a $6.46 million war chest and 92% in-state donors. He is leaving Congress not because he served the people of the 6th District, but because he found a bigger office to not serve them from.

Rep. Chip Roy is the Freedom Caucus's favorite attack dog — a former Ted Cruz chief of staff who built his brand on theatrical confrontation with Republican leadership, then parlayed that brand into a run for Texas Attorney General. He is Congress's most entertaining fiscal scold, the guy who will hold up a spending bill for three days while delivering a floor speech about the national debt, then vote for a defense authorization that adds hundreds of billions to it. He is, depending on your perspective, either a principled constitutionalist or a man who mistakes obstruction for governance.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Roy deserves genuine credit on exactly one: banning congressional stock trading. He co-authored the TRUST in Congress Act with Democrat Seth Magaziner, a bipartisan bill that would force members, spouses, and dependents to divest individual stocks or place them in blind trusts. He reintroduced it in 2025, and it remains one of the strongest stock-trading ban proposals in Congress. On this single issue, Roy is ahead of most of his colleagues in both parties.

And that is approximately where the good news ends. Roy is the policy chair of the House Freedom Caucus, which means he spends most of his energy fighting over the size of spending bills rather than the substance of reform. He opposed raising the debt ceiling in December 2024, drawing a public rebuke from Donald Trump, who encouraged Texas Republicans to primary him — a threat Roy shrugged off before announcing his AG campaign. He fought the "One Big Beautiful Bill" over Inflation Reduction Act tax credits, which he branded the "green new scam," alongside fellow Texan Keith Self. Noble-sounding fiscal hawkery, except Roy's crusade was about killing clean energy incentives, not funding Social Security or negotiating drug prices.

On the remaining 80% Bill policies, Roy's record ranges from hostile to nonexistent. He has never supported the DISCLOSE Act, dark money bans, or corporate PAC reform. He voted against condemning Russia's abduction of Ukrainian children — one of only nine Republicans to do so. He joined the "Sharia Free America Caucus" with Keith Self, which tells you something about where his priorities land. His League of Conservation Voters score is effectively zero. He joined bipartisan bills the third-least of any House member, according to GovTrack's 2024 report card.

On Pentagon auditing, ending forever wars, Medicare drug negotiation, taxing the ultra-wealthy, fair elections, protecting farmland, banning corporate home buying, body cameras, paid family leave, the Epstein files, closing tax loopholes, right to repair, banning junk fees, and term limits, Roy has either voted against relevant legislation or maintained conspicuous silence. His Heritage Action score confirms what the voting record shows: he is a reliable movement conservative who occasionally bucks leadership on process, never on substance.

Roy's constituents in TX-21 — stretching from Austin's suburbs through the Hill Country to San Antonio — support all twenty of these policies. Roy gives them one. The other nineteen, apparently, are someone else's problem. Possibly the next attorney general's.

Delia C. Ramirez represents Illinois's 3rd Congressional District, a sprawling stretch of Chicago's West Side and western suburbs that she has held since winning her seat in the 2022 midterms [1]. A former state legislator and affordable housing advocate, Ramirez arrived in Washington as part of the Congressional Progressive Caucus and quickly established herself as one of the more outspoken freshman members on economic justice issues [2]. She is the first Latina to represent Illinois in Congress, and she has leaned into that identity as a lens for policy, particularly on housing, immigration, and working-class economics.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Ramirez checks a remarkable number of boxes. She cosponsored the ETHICS Act to ban congressional stock trading [3], signed on to the Ban Corporate PACs Act [4], and has been a vocal supporter of Medicare drug price negotiation under the Inflation Reduction Act framework [5]. She cosponsored the AUMF Repeal Act to end the open-ended war authorizations that have lingered since 2001 and 2002 [6], and backed the DISCLOSE Act to drag dark money into the sunlight [7]. On housing, she has been one of the loudest voices in Congress pushing to ban corporate purchases of single-family homes, introducing the End Hedge Fund Control of American Homes Act alongside fellow progressives [8]. She supports paid family leave, has called for fully funding Social Security, and cosponsored the Fair Elections Now Act to publicly finance campaigns [9].

Where it gets more nuanced is on the issues that test progressive orthodoxy. Ramirez has not cosponsored congressional term limits legislation, a position that quietly aligns with the institutional preferences of House Democratic leadership [10]. She has been relatively quiet on the Pentagon audit question, despite the Department of Defense failing its sixth consecutive audit in 2023 [11]. On the right to repair, she has not taken a public legislative position, though her consumer-protection instincts suggest the issue simply has not reached her radar rather than active opposition.

Follow the money and the picture stays relatively clean for a freshman. Ramirez's top contributors in her first cycle were labor unions, including SEIU, AFSCME, and the Laborers' International Union, alongside small-dollar individual donors [12]. She does not accept corporate PAC money, a pledge she made during her campaign and has maintained in office [4]. Her fundraising profile is notably modest compared to colleagues who arrived in the same class, which either reflects principled restraint or the reality that a D+30 district does not require a war chest [13].

The bottom line on Ramirez is that she is one of the closer fits to the 80% Bill in the House. Her record on stock trading bans, dark money, corporate PAC refusal, housing, drug pricing, and war powers is substantive and documented. The gaps — term limits, Pentagon accountability, right to repair — are sins of omission rather than opposition. She is, for now, more aligned with what 80% of Americans say they want than the vast majority of her colleagues, which is either a testament to her convictions or an indictment of everyone else's.

Pete Ricketts is Nebraska's junior U.S. Senator, appointed in January 2023 and elected in the 2024 special election with 62.5% of the vote [1]. But before he was a senator, he was the governor of Nebraska, and before that, he was the son of Joe Ricketts, the billionaire founder of TD Ameritrade. The Ricketts family owns the Chicago Cubs and has poured over $100 million into federal races since 2005, much of it through super PACs [2][3]. Pete himself holds an estimated $213 million in assets, including up to $25 million in Charles Schwab stock — the company that bought TD Ameritrade for $26 billion [4][5]. He is, by any measure, one of the wealthiest members of the Senate, and he legislates accordingly.

To his credit, Ricketts has been a vocal champion of Protecting US Farmland from foreign ownership, introducing the AFIDA Improvements Act with Democrat John Fetterman to crack down on Chinese purchases of American agricultural land [6][7]. He also eventually voted to Release the Epstein Files in November 2025, though only after first voting to kill a Democratic motion that would have released them months earlier — a convenient flip once the political winds shifted [8][9]. On Congressional Term Limits, he has made no meaningful push despite the idea polling above 80% with voters.

On the rest of the 80% Bill, Ricketts is a fortress of opposition. He has not supported Banning Congressional Stock Trading — a particularly awkward position for a man sitting on $126 million in publicly traded assets who made an estimated $10 million in stock market gains in a single month [10][11]. He has shown no support for the DISCLOSE Act or Banning Dark Money, which is hardly surprising given that his family founded and funded the Ending Spending Action Fund, a super PAC to which Joe and Marlene Ricketts have donated nearly $36.6 million, alongside a 501(c)(4) dark money arm that doesn't disclose its donors [12][13]. On Medicare Drug Negotiation, Taxing the Ultra-Wealthy, Auditing the Pentagon, Banning Corporate PACs, Fair Elections, Paid Family Leave, Police Body Cameras, Closing Tax Loopholes, Right to Repair, Banning Junk Fees, Funding Social Security, a Lifetime Lobbying Ban, Ending Forever Wars, and Banning Corporate Purchase of Single Family Homes — Ricketts has been either silent or opposed.

Follow the money and the picture is almost comically on-the-nose. Ricketts raised $4.66 million for his 2024 campaign, with $600,000 of the associated Ricketts Victory Fund coming from his own parents [14][15]. Since 2005, Joe, Marlene, and Pete Ricketts have combined to spend more than $100 million on federal elections [3]. When your family has personally bankrolled a dark money super PAC and you hold nine figures in stock, voting to Ban Dark Money, Ban Congressional Stock Trading, or Tax the Ultra-Wealthy would be legislating against yourself.

The bottom line on Pete Ricketts: he is a billionaire's son with a billionaire's portfolio who votes like a billionaire's senator. He will protect Nebraska farmland from China but won't protect Nebraska voters from the dark money machine his own family built. The 80% Bill asks for reforms that 80% of Americans support — but when you're worth $213 million and your family has spent $100 million keeping the system exactly the way it is, that 80% is someone else's problem.

Michael Rulli is a Republican congressman representing Ohio's 6th district, a sprawling stretch of Appalachian and Rust Belt communities along the state's eastern border. Before arriving in Congress via a June 2024 special election, Rulli spent five years in the Ohio State Senate and a career running Rulli Bros. Markets, his family's grocery chain in the Mahoning Valley [1]. He won his seat with a funding mix that tells its own story: 41% of his campaign dollars came from PACs and another 51% from large individual contributions, while small donors under $200 accounted for just 3.4% of his war chest [2].

Rulli does check a few 80% Bill boxes. He signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge and cosponsored HJR 11 and HJR 12, constitutional amendments to impose congressional term limits — a position supported by roughly 82% of Americans [3]. He sits on the Energy and Commerce Committee, with subcommittee assignments in Energy, Health, and Oversight and Investigations, giving him direct jurisdiction over drug pricing, right to repair, junk fees, and data privacy — yet he has not introduced or cosponsored legislation on any of those topics [4]. He also serves on the Education and Workforce Committee, which handles paid family leave, another 80% Bill priority he has not addressed.

On transparency, Rulli's record is thin. He was one of only two Republicans who did not vote on the Epstein Files Transparency Act in November 2025 — though in fairness, he was absent caring for his father, who passed away on November 25 [5]. He has taken no public position on banning congressional stock trading, the DISCLOSE Act, or overturning Citizens United. His public statements have focused on border security, the SAVE Act for election integrity, and supporting DOGE — priorities that, whatever their merits, do not appear anywhere in the 80% Bill [6].

Rulli's top donors include Pi&I Motor Express ($22,500) and a collection of corporate PACs including American Crystal Sugar, TTM Technology, and SpaceX [2]. For a grocery store owner who presumably understands what junk fees and drug prices do to working families, the silence on kitchen-table 80% Bill issues is notable. He talks term limits — the safest populist stance in politics — but on stock trading bans, dark money disclosure, Pentagon audits, Social Security funding, and corporate home-buying, the congressman from Ohio's 6th has nothing on the record.
